memes.label: Acts
memes.description: Index of all five acts of The School for Scandal with scene counts, key characters, and dramatic functions.

memes.label: Annuity
memes.description: The financial instrument of the annuity as a plot device exposing the economic desperation and moral choices of characters in The School for Scandal

memes.label: Appearance
memes.description: The governing theme of Sheridan's satire — the gap between how things seem and how they are, encoded in the Surface brothers' very name and tested in every scene of the play

memes.label: Appearance vs. Reality
memes.description: The governing philosophical question of the play — how to distinguish genuine virtue from its performance, encoded in the Surface brothers' surname and tested through disguise, concealment, and revelation in every major scene

memes.label: "Auction"
memes.description: "The auction of the Surface family portraits in Act IV — one of the great comic set pieces in English drama."

memes.label: "Bath"
memes.description: "The spa town of Bath — setting for off-stage scandal and a key location in 18th-century social life."

memes.label: "Beaux"
memes.description: "The fashionable male type — Sir Benjamin Backbite as the beau of The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Benevolence
memes.description: A key virtue in 18th-century moral philosophy, invoked and subverted throughout the play — the test that distinguishes genuine from counterfeit virtue

memes.label: Calumny
memes.description: The moral and social dimensions of calumny — false and malicious misrepresentation — in The School for Scandal.

memes.label: Candour
memes.description: The ideal of frank, open speech and its ironic corruption in the scandal-mongering world of The School for Scandal

memes.label: Careles
memes.description: Charles Surface's companion in dissipation — the friend whose very name ("Careless") announces his function as a mirror for Charles's own carelessness, and whose presence in the drinking and auction scenes provides comic camaraderie

memes.label: Characters
memes.description: Complete index of all characters in The School for Scandal, sorted alphabetically with quality grades.

memes.label: Charity
memes.description: The virtue of charitable giving in Sheridan's comedy — a moral test that exposes the difference between Joseph Surface's professions and Charles Surface's actions

memes.label: Charles Surface
memes.description: The "wild" brother whose extravagance conceals genuine virtue — Sheridan's argument that a good heart matters more than a good reputation, tested and vindicated in the play's most famous scenes

memes.label: "Club"
memes.description: "The gentlemen's club as scene of gossip and conviviality in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: "Comedy of Manners"
memes.description: "The theatrical genre to which The School for Scandal belongs, focusing on the follies and affectations of fashionable society."

memes.label: Concepts
memes.description: Complete index of all concepts explored in The School for Scandal, sorted alphabetically with quality grades.

memes.label: Confidence
memes.description: The act of entrusting secrets or reliance to another — in The School for Scandal, confidence is both the currency of intimacy and the weapon of betrayal

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The internal moral arbiter that Joseph Surface famously dismisses and Lady Teazle ultimately heeds — conscience marks the boundary between those who can be saved and those who cannot

memes.label: "Coquette"
memes.description: "The flirtatious woman — Lady Teazle's early role as coquette in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: "Cosmetic"
memes.description: "Cosmetics and artifice — Lady Sneerwell at her dressing table as emblem of deception."

memes.label: "Country"
memes.description: "The country estate as moral counterpoint to London corruption in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Crabtree
memes.description: Sir Benjamin Backbite's uncle and fellow scandal-monger — the older generation of the gossip economy, proving that the "School for Scandal" has a faculty as well as students

memes.label: Credit
memes.description: The twin meanings Sheridan exploits — financial creditworthiness and social credibility — bound together in a society where a person's word is their bond and their bond is their reputation

memes.label: "Curricle"
memes.description: "The fashionable carriage of the 1770s — a status symbol referenced in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Debt
memes.description: The financial and moral condition that Sheridan uses to test character — debt as the visible trace of vice in Charles and the invisible hypocrisy in Joseph, and as the mechanism through which the play's moral accounting operates

memes.label: "Demirep"
memes.description: "A woman of dubious reputation in 18th-century parlance — the social category Lady Brittle exemplifies."

memes.label: Dissipation
memes.description: The life of pleasure and excess associated with Charles Surface — dissipation is the visible sign of moral looseness that conceals an invisible moral core

memes.label: "Dowry"
memes.description: "The marriage portion — Maria's financial situation and the economics of marriage in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: "Drury Lane Theatre"
memes.description: "The theatre where The School for Scandal premiered on 8 May 1777."

memes.label: "Duel"
memes.description: "The code of honour and its role in the play's resolution — Sir Peter's challenge to Charles and the reconciliation it precipitates."

memes.label: Enemy
memes.description: The label that masks and reveals relationships in Sheridan's comedy — calling someone an enemy is as often a tactic of concealment as a statement of fact

memes.label: "Epilogue"
memes.description: "The epilogue to The School for Scandal — closing the play's moral argument."

memes.label: Extravagance
memes.description: The conspicuous consumption that defines Charles Surface's character and provides the surface pretext for the scandal that engulfs him — a vice that the play ultimately treats more leniently than hypocrisy

memes.label: Family
memes.description: The network of obligation, inheritance, and identity that Sheridan tests throughout the play — family as the structure that both constrains and reveals character

memes.label: Fashion
memes.description: The tyrannical code of dress, manners, and consumption that governs London society in Sheridan's satire — a measure of status that Lady Teazle embraces and Sir Peter despises

memes.label: First Gentleman
memes.description: An unnamed gentleman in Charles Surface's social circle who participates in the Act III drinking scene, encouraging revelry and the prank on Sir Oliver Surface disguised as Mr. Premium.

memes.label: Fortune
memes.description: The double meaning — wealth and luck — that Sheridan exploits throughout the play, where inherited fortune and personal misfortune intertwine in the moral testing of characters and the play's comic resolution

memes.label: Friendship and Society
memes.description: The role of friendship in the social world of The School for Scandal — its uses, abuses, and performative nature in London high society.

memes.label: "Front Matter"
memes.description: "Dramatis Personae and introductory content for The School for Scandal"

memes.label: "Gallantry"
memes.description: "The code of gallant behaviour and its subversion in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: "David Garrick"
memes.description: "David Garrick — the actor-manager who wrote the prologue and championed Sheridan's early career."

memes.label: "Gossip"
memes.description: "The mechanism of scandal circulation in 18th-century London society, and the engine of The School for Scandal's plot."

memes.label: Gratitude
memes.description: The duty of acknowledging a benefit — in Sheridan's comedy, gratitude is the moral test that distinguishes Charles Surface from Joseph, and sincerity from performance

memes.label: Guardian
memes.description: The legal and moral authority over a dependent's marriage — Sir Peter's guardianship of Maria is the play's central contested power relationship

memes.label: Happiness
memes.description: The elusive emotional goal of every character — happiness in The School for Scandal is what the scandalous pursue, the virtuous deserve, and the hypocritical pretend to have

memes.label: Heir
memes.description: Inheritance and heirship as a driving force of plot and character motivation in The School for Scandal.

memes.label: "Heiress"
memes.description: "Maria as heiress — her fortune and the marriage-market dynamics in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: The code of personal integrity that the play's characters invoke, manipulate, and betray — a contested concept at the intersection of morality and social performance

memes.label: Husband
memes.description: The comic predicament of married men in Sheridan's play — legally empowered but socially outmanoeuvred, a figure of authority perpetually on the back foot

memes.label: Hypocrisy
memes.description: The central moral target of Sheridan's satire — the gap between professed virtue and actual conduct, embodied most fully in Joseph Surface

memes.label: Hypocrisy and Deception
memes.description: The moral dimension of false appearance — hypocrisy as distinct from social masking, centred on Joseph Surface's performance of virtue for predatory ends

memes.label: Innocence
memes.description: The paradoxical vulnerability of innocence in a world of scandal — how consciousness of one's own innocence becomes a liability in The School for Scandal

memes.label: "Intrigue"
memes.description: "Intrigue as plot mechanism — scheming, letters, and plotting in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Jealousy
memes.description: A destructive force in marital relations — Sir Peter's jealousy of Lady Teazle is both the symptom and the cause of their estrangement in The School for Scandal

memes.label: "Jeweller"
memes.description: "The jeweller as minor character in The School for Scandal — credit, commerce, and the fashionable economy."

memes.label: Joseph Surface
memes.description: The "man of sentiment" whose every virtue is a performance — Sheridan's definitive portrait of the hypocrite as social artist, and the character who gives the play its satirical edge

memes.label: Lady Sneerwell
memes.description: The architect of the play's scandal machinery — a widow who weaponises gossip from her dressing table, running what amounts to a rumour factory that targets every reputation in London

memes.label: Lady Teazle
memes.description: The country girl turned fashionable wife whose journey from rural innocence through metropolitan affectation to recovered integrity forms the play's emotional and moral core — she is the character who learns the School for Scandal is a school one can graduate from

memes.label: "Lappet"
memes.description: "Lady Sneerwell's maid, present at her dressing table in the opening scene of The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Libertine
memes.description: The libertine as a stock character type in Restoration and eighteenth-century comedy, and its reformation through Charles Surface in The School for Scandal

memes.label: London
memes.description: The social stage on which Sheridan's comedy unfolds — London is more than setting; it is the condition of possibility for the scandal economy and the yardstick of fashionable life

memes.label: Love
memes.description: The emotion that Sheridan treats with tactical seriousness beneath the comedy — love as the test that reveals character, the bond that survives satire, and the condition the play's moral economy rewards

memes.label: Lover
memes.description: The socially codified role of the romantic pursuer in eighteenth-century London society — a performance of sentiment that may mask fortune-hunting, vanity, or genuine attachment

memes.label: "Luxury"
memes.description: "Conspicuous consumption as a marker of status and a moral test in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Malice
memes.description: The deliberate infliction of harm through speech and action, a driving force behind the scandal machinery in The School for Scandal

memes.label: Maria
memes.description: Lady Sneerwell's ward and the play's romantic centre — the young woman whose hand in marriage is contested by the Surface brothers and whose steadfast preference for Charles over Joseph prefigures the play's moral judgment

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: Marriage as a social institution, plot device, and subject of satire in The School for Scandal.

memes.label: Marriage and Gender
memes.description: The play's extended examination of marriage as a social institution — the power dynamics between husbands and wives, the double standard of sexual reputation, and the comic possibility of reconciliation between the sexes

memes.label: "Marriage Market"
memes.description: "The 18th-century marriage market as social institution — commerce and courtship in the play."

memes.label: "Masquerade"
memes.description: "Disguise and deception — Sir Oliver's masquerade as Premium and Stanley in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Mercy
memes.description: The act of forgiving in Sheridan's comedy — a virtue that Sir Oliver extends to Charles, Sir Peter extends to Lady Teazle, and Joseph Surface conspicuously fails to practise

memes.label: Misfortune
memes.description: The undeserved hardships that test character — Sheridan uses misfortune to distinguish between those who exploit suffering and those who alleviate it

memes.label: Miss Verjuice
memes.description: Lady Sneerwell's cousin and confidante, whose name suggests the sour, acidic quality of her conversation and character.

memes.label: Moses
memes.description: The moneylender who facilitates Charles Surface's borrowing — a minor comic figure whose professional cynicism ("when a man wants money, where the plague should he get it?") captures the play's pragmatic attitude toward finance

memes.label: Mrs. Candour
memes.description: The scandal-monger whose name is Sheridan's most savage irony — "Candour" means honesty, but Mrs. Candour's candour consists of pretending to defend reputations while systematically destroying them

memes.label: Mrs. Clackit
memes.description: An off-stage scandal-monger whose talent for spreading damaging information makes her a central node in the play's gossip network.

memes.label: "Nabob"
memes.description: "East India Company fortune — Sir Oliver Surface's backstory and colonial wealth in the play."

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The innate moral character that governs behaviour — in The School for Scandal, the tension between good nature and ill nature drives character judgment and social perception

memes.label: Neighbour
memes.description: The neighbour as both witness and victim — in The School for Scandal, proximity is the condition of vulnerability to gossip

memes.label: "Opera"
memes.description: "The opera as social venue in 18th-century London — a setting referenced in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: "Pall Mall"
memes.description: "London's fashionable street — a key location in 18th-century social geography."

memes.label: "Paragraph"
memes.description: "Newspaper paragraphs as vehicle for scandal-mongering in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Passion
memes.description: The force that drives characters into error in Sheridan's comedy — passion is both the engine of the scandal plot and the feeling that must be mastered before resolution can arrive

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: The pursuit of enjoyment in Sheridan's comedy — a social activity that distinguishes Charles Surface's genuine conviviality from the calculated performances of the scandal school

memes.label: Portrait
memes.description: The physical object that becomes the play's most powerful symbol — the family portrait that Charles refuses to sell, transforming a prop into a moral test and a theatrical emblem of loyalty

memes.label: "Portrait Gallery"
memes.description: "Charles Surface's family portraits and the auction scene — art, ancestry, and identity."

memes.label: Power
memes.description: The currency of control in the scandal economy — exercised through information, patronage, and seduction rather than force

memes.label: "Prodigal"
memes.description: "The figure of the prodigal son, embodied by Charles Surface — the wastrel whose good heart redeems his bad habits."

memes.label: "Prologue"
memes.description: "David Garrick's prologue to The School for Scandal — an important framing text for the play."

memes.label: Prudence
memes.description: The worldly virtue that Sheridan's play treats with deep ambivalence — praised by the respectable as a safeguard against ruin, yet exposed as a mask for selfishness when practised without generosity

memes.label: Quarrel
memes.description: The domestic warfare that defines the Teazles' marriage — in Sheridan's comedy, quarrelling is a language of intimacy as much as a symptom of discord

memes.label: "Rake"
memes.description: "The Restoration rake figure and its transformation in Sheridan's hands — Charles Surface as reformed libertine."

memes.label: Reform
memes.description: The possibility of moral change — a central question in The School for Scandal, where the play asks whether a dissolute character can truly reform and what genuine moral improvement requires

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: The social currency that drives the plot of The School for Scandal — characters scheme, deceive, and ruin one another over the preservation or destruction of reputation

memes.label: "Restoration Comedy"
memes.description: "The dramatic tradition preceding and influencing Sheridan's The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Rowley
memes.description: Sir Peter's steward and the play's most reliable truth-teller — the character who sees through Joseph's performance before anyone else and who quietly arranges the testing that will expose him

memes.label: Ruin
memes.description: The spectre of financial and social destruction that haunts every character in The School for Scandal — the fate that scandal, extravagance, and false friendship conspire to produce

memes.label: Scandal
memes.description: The central satirical target of Sheridan's play — the social machinery of rumour, reputation-destruction, and hypocritical moralising in 18th-century London society

memes.label: "Scandal Sheet"
memes.description: "The printed scandal sheets and newspapers of 18th-century London — the medium of Lady Sneerwell's trade."

memes.label: Scandal and Gossip
memes.description: The central social mechanism of the play — the production, circulation, and consumption of scandalous narratives as a form of social currency that organises, rewards, and ultimately consumes its practitioners

memes.label: Scandalous
memes.description: The social meaning of the label "scandalous" in The School for Scandal — how the accusation of scandalousness is itself an act of scandal.

memes.label: Screen
memes.description: The most famous prop in English comedy — the screen behind which characters hide and truths are revealed, transforming a piece of furniture into the engine of the play's climactic unmasking

memes.label: "Season"
memes.description: "The London social season — temporal frame and social rhythm in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Second Gentleman
memes.description: An unnamed gentleman in Charles Surface's social circle who participates in the Act III drinking scene, supporting the revelry and the mockery of Sir Oliver Surface disguised as Mr. Premium.

memes.label: Sentiment
memes.description: The 18th-century cult of sensibility that Sheridan simultaneously employs and satirises — the performance of refined emotion as social credential, and the central test of character in a world where feeling can be counterfeited

memes.label: Sentiment and Sensibility
memes.description: The cult of sentiment in eighteenth-century culture and its theatrical critique — how Sheridan exposes the gap between performed feeling and genuine virtue

memes.label: "Sentimental Comedy"
memes.description: "The rival 18th-century theatrical genre that Sheridan satirises through the character of Joseph Surface."

memes.label: "Servant"
memes.description: "A minor character in The School for Scandal — a servant at Joseph Surface's house who announces visitors."

memes.label: "Richard Brinsley Sheridan"
memes.description: "The playwright — biography, context, and the creation of The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Sir Benjamin Backbite
memes.description: The would-be poet and would-be lover whose very name announces his dramatic function — a comic figure who "bites" with his verses and his gossip, pursuing Maria with execrable elegies while feeding the scandal mill

memes.label: Sir Oliver Surface
memes.description: The returning uncle whose disguises as moneylender and poor relation transform him into the play's moral testing apparatus — the character who sees through surfaces by refusing to trust them

memes.label: Sir Peter Teazle
memes.description: The aging husband and guardian whose attempts to navigate a marriage of December and May, and to discern between his two nephews, drive the play's central moral investigations

memes.label: Site Index
memes.description: Master index of all wiki entries for The School for Scandal, grouped by page type with quality grades and reference counts.

memes.label: Slander
memes.description: The weaponised speech that drives the plot — distinct from mere gossip in its intent to destroy, and gendered in its application, with female reputations singled out for particular vulnerability

memes.label: Snake
memes.description: Lady Sneerwell's paid agent — the forger and rumour-monger whose eventual betrayal of his employer provides the plot mechanism for the play's resolution, demonstrating that even the scandal economy's operators can be bought

memes.label: Society
memes.description: The "world" that Sheridan anatomises — fashionable London as a system of performance, surveillance, and mutual destruction that the play simultaneously entertains and indicts

memes.label: "Stanley (Mr. Stanley)"
memes.description: "The alias adopted by Sir Oliver Surface to test Joseph's charity — a poor relation seeking financial aid."

memes.label: "Subscription"
memes.description: "The subscription model for charity, publications, and opera boxes in 18th-century London."

memes.label: "Surgeon"
memes.description: "The wound and surgeon motif — Sir Peter's wound and the duel's aftermath in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Tale
memes.description: The unit of currency in the scandal economy — the "Tale of Scandal" that Sheridan anatomises as both a social weapon and a narrative form, with its own conventions, authors, and audiences

memes.label: Taste
memes.description: The faculty of aesthetic and social discernment in Sheridan's comedy — a marker of status that Lady Teazle adopts, Sir Peter mocks, and Charles Surface possesses in a form that commerce can recognise

memes.label: Temper
memes.description: The barometer of social harmony in Sheridan's comedy — a quality that Sir Peter and Lady Teazle wrestle over as both personal failing and marital weapon

memes.label: "The Screen"
memes.description: "The famous screen scene in Act IV — the comic and dramatic climax of The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Themes
memes.description: Complete index of all thematic analyses for The School for Scandal, sorted alphabetically with quality grades.

memes.label: "Town"
memes.description: "London 'Town' as social arena — fashion, gossip, and the Season in The School for Scandal."

memes.label: Town and Country
memes.description: The structural opposition between metropolitan corruption and rural innocence that organises the play's moral geography — the "Town" as the site of the School for Scandal, the "Country" as the memory of uncorrupted virtue

memes.label: Trip
memes.description: Charles Surface's footman — a minor comic figure whose pretensions to gentility mirror and parody his master's extravagance, proving that the "School for Scandal" educates servants as thoroughly as masters

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: The most elusive commodity in the scandal economy — constantly invoked but rarely spoken, and often the last thing anyone wishes to hear

memes.label: "Tunbridge Wells"
memes.description: "Tunbridge Wells — the spa resort mentioned in the play's scandal anecdotes."

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: Public moral excellence and its counterfeits — in The School for Scandal, virtue is the quality everyone claims and few possess

memes.label: "Wager"
memes.description: "Betting and wagering as social practice in The School for Scandal — the gambling culture of 18th-century London."

memes.label: Ward
memes.description: The legal and social institution of wardship — and its implications for female autonomy — as dramatised through Maria's position in The School for Scandal

memes.label: Wealth and Class
memes.description: The play's anatomy of the relationship between money and morality — how wealth is acquired, circulated, and distributed, and what a person's attitude toward money reveals about their character

memes.label: Widow
memes.description: The social position of the widow in The School for Scandal — Lady Sneerwell's status as a young widow grants her both freedom and vulnerability within the scandal economy

memes.label: "William"
memes.description: "A servant in The School for Scandal who appears briefly in Joseph Surface's household."

memes.label: Wit
memes.description: The weapon of choice in Sheridan's comedy of manners — verbal cleverness as social performance, as tool of malice, and as index of character in a world where what one says is inseparable from who one is

memes.label: Wit and Comedy
memes.description: The role of wit as social performance and weapon in Sheridan's comedy of manners — verbal brilliance as both social currency and agent of exposure

memes.label: Wound
memes.description: The physical and symbolic wound in The School for Scandal — from the duel to the postman, wounds expose the absurdity of scandal's escalation

memes.label: Youth
memes.description: Youth as a period of moral testing in The School for Scandal — characters are judged by how they use or abuse the privileges and dangers of being young
